exercises.title: A: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.

items.sentence: (1) ...... (WORK) at Riverside General Hospital gathered outside the main entrance today to protest against proposed budget cuts. Union spokesperson Claire Henderson told reporters: 'We have no intention of calling an immediate walkout, but our members will refuse to do (2) ...... (TIME) shifts until this matter is resolved. We are calling on (3) ...... (MANAGE) to withdraw these plans without delay.'

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is plural WORK + overtime + MANAGE, so the correct answer is "Workers, overtime, management".

wrongOptions.1: The bare infinitive (1) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: The ongoing dispute, now in its fourth week, has severely disrupted (4) ...... (PRODUCE) at the facility. Dr Marcus Holt, Chief Executive of the hospital trust, has stood firm. 'To remain (5) ...... (COMPETE) in attracting top medical professionals, we must modernise our operations,' he stated.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun PRODUCE + adjective COMPETE, so the correct answer is "production, competitive".

wrongOptions.4: The bare infinitive (4) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: 'As an (6) ...... (EMPLOY) responsible for more than 600 (7) ...... (EMPLOY), I have a duty to keep this institution financially sustainable. (8) ...... (PERIOD), all organisations must adapt to shifting circumstances and demands.'

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is eMPLOY forms + periodic adverb, so the correct answer is "employer, employees, Periodically".

wrongOptions.6: The bare infinitive (6) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: Several of the administrative procedures currently in use are, quite simply, (9) ...... (ANTIQUE) and entirely (10) ...... (APPLY) in today's healthcare environment.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is outdated and not applicable, so the correct answer is "antiquated, inapplicable".

wrongOptions.9: The bare infinitive (9) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

exercises.title: B: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
The new ointment was incredibly effective and the rash disappeared overnight. **MAGIC**
The new ointment ...... and the rash disappeared overnight.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is very effective, so the correct answer is "worked like magic".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "worked like magic", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
Things went brilliantly from the very first moment when the café opened its doors. **FLYING**
The café ...... when it opened its doors for the first time.
items.answer: got off to a flying start
acceptedAnswers: got off to a flying start

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is great start, so the correct answer is "got off to a flying start".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "got off to a flying start", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I do enjoy working from home, but I sometimes miss the social side of the office. **AGAIN**
I do enjoy working from home, but ...... I miss the social side of the office.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is on the other hand, so the correct answer is "then again".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "then again", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
The new principal was committed to stopping all forms of cheating in the school. **END**
The new principal was committed to ...... to all forms of cheating in the school.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is stop something, so the correct answer is "put an end".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "put an end", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
Hardly anyone now truly believes in astrology, do they? **AGE**
Hardly anyone ...... truly believes in astrology, do they?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is nowadays, so the correct answer is "in this day and age".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "in this day and age", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
We had almost boarded the plane when the gate agent announced a delay. **POINT**
We were ...... the plane when the gate agent announced a delay.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is about to do something, so the correct answer is "on the point of boarding".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "on the point of boarding", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I just need to sign this form and then I'll be ready to go. **SECOND**
It ...... sign this form and then I'll be ready to go.
items.answer: will only take a second to
acceptedAnswers: will only take a second to

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is very quick, so the correct answer is "will only take a second to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "will only take a second to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Clara would cancel all her plans immediately if something more exciting came up. **HAT**
Clara would cancel all her plans ...... if something more exciting came up.
items.answer: at the drop of a hat
acceptedAnswers: at the drop of a hat

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is immediately, so the correct answer is "at the drop of a hat".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "at the drop of a hat", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: C: Phrasal Verbs
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: We've been completely snowed ...... with customer complaints since the new product launched.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is overwhelmed, so the correct answer is "under".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: I've borrowed a little cash from my flatmate to tide me ...... until my salary comes in.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is until payday, so the correct answer is "over".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "over" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "I've borrowed a little cash from my flatmate to tide me ...... until my salary c…".

items.sentence: An urgent matter has cropped ...... and I won't be able to make it to training tonight.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is appeared unexpectedly, so the correct answer is "up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "up" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "An urgent matter has cropped ...... and I won't be able to make it to training t…".

items.sentence: The director has decided not to take ...... any new projects until the current workload eases.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is accept new work, so the correct answer is "on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: She could while the entire weekend ...... browsing through vintage bookshops.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is pass time leisurely, so the correct answer is "away".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: The builders usually knock ...... at around five o'clock on Fridays.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is finish work, so the correct answer is "off".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Despite strong resistance from several board members, the CEO intends to press ...... with the restructuring plan.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is continue despite resistance, so the correct answer is "on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: Nearly two hundred staff were laid ...... after the airline announced it was closing three regional hubs.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is made redundant, so the correct answer is "off".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "off" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "Nearly two hundred staff were laid ...... after the airline announced it was clo…".

exercises.title: D: Error Correction (Future Tenses)
exercises.instructions: If the word or phrase in bold is correct, put OK. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly on the line.
exercises.studentInstruction: If the word or phrase in bold is correct, tap OK. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: This time tomorrow I'll sit on a beach somewhere in the south of Spain.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.sit: "sit" is the highlighted error — use "will be sitting" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "sit", not elsewhere — replace it with "will be sitting" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: This time tomorrow I'll <mistake>sit</mistake> on a beach somewhere in the south of Spain.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: This time tomorrow I'll will be sitting on a beach somewhere in the south of Spain.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: This time tomorrow I'll will be sitting on a beach somewhere in the south of Spain.

wrongOptions.you'll have finished: "you'll have finished" is the highlighted error — use "OK" here instead.

explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Do you think OK the report by the time the director calls?

items.sentence: Will I make some tea while you get yourself settled?

wrongOptions.Will I: "Will I" is the highlighted error — use "Shall I" here instead.
